sentences.text: I'll go back here for a little bit to this Polish gentleman who helped us tremendously during-- during the rough times in the camps.
I indicated that he wanted to hide me in-- in-- We-- we hear stories, I mean, there-- there-- there were-- there were just enough Poles that wanted to do this.
And yet, this man knew what the end would be if-- if they ever would discover me in his place.
But they knew that the war was going badly for the Germans.
And it was a matter of-- of a few months before the liberation, and before the Russians were actually-- they occupied.
They-- they came.
They were the first to occupy our camp.
So the camp also employed a lot of civilian people there.
And this is where Mr.
Kolojejiik came in as one of the workers in there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to survive in camp, of course, we also had selections, which means Mengele came into the camp.
Now, Mengele was closer to me than you are.
And he was right there, looking you straight in your eyes.
And of course, they looked at you to see whether you still had enough flesh on your bones.
Otherwise, you'd go to one side again.
And when he was done, I mean, they would always take away truckloads of people, because there were new arrivals coming in.
And they were probably-- they could last longer than the ones which already worked to the bone.
That's exactly the definition-- worked to the bone.
There was nothing but bones in there.
And there was a name for these people.
They called them Muselmanns.
Muselmanns are usually thin people.
And-- and this-- this is where they would take them away.
So Mengele was right there in front of me.
I faced him twice.
I guess God must have wanted me to live for a certain purpose.
I don't know why.
But I don't know yet why that he left me.
He allowed me to live.
And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the camp also had a choir.
The Germans loved to be entertained.
And they had some great musicians-- choirmasters, professors.
I mean, they had-- which were lowered to nothing.
I mean, they were just garbage truckers.
They were cleaning up the camps.
And we would get-- we would get a quart of extra soup if we would come to practice, and then perform for the-- for the Nazis, for the-- for the SS and their concert.
They also had boxing in the camps.
We had a boxer in our camp, a-- a Greek boxer who was famous in Greece.
I don't remember his name.
And of course, he made that mistake by beating the SS boxer.
They figure they can beat anyone, but he happened to have been a professional from Greece.
And this was towards the later years.
This was about 1944, when they brought in the Greek people, towards the end, the Greek Jews in there.
But in order to survive in the camps, you-- you had to somehow keep clean.
A lot of people died from, really, from-- from-- from-- from not being clean, not trying to wash.
We had no-- I mean, we couldn't go bathing.
But you had a wash room where everybody would go in.
There was cold water.
You could wash.
Once, I do not remember, once a week, once a month, you would be taken in to shower, to a shower room.
And you could shower.
And one of the guys who was an inmate also, who was operating the showers, saw me every morning.
I was running to be one of the first ones to wash.
And he told me, he says, Ben, he says, you know, you come so early.
He says, you can come in.
I mean, there were a lot of people which were taking showers in the morning, privileged ones, or there might be some SS, he says.
But we have some of the people which are operating the barracks which are all day in the barracks.
They-- they come and take a shower too, he says.
And from then on, I could take a shower almost any time in the morning first.
So cleanliness was a part of your survival, cleanliness on top of it.
Also, of course, the longer you were in the camps, I mentioned a-- a-- it just came to my mind the-- the incident.
Yes, I was-- it was curfew time.
And whether I was running, before my brother joined me or so, joined our-- our kommando, I was late.
Curfew, for example, is six o'clock.
And anyone seen after six o'clock, I mean, you got problems and trouble.
And the-- lagerkommandant we called them-- he was also an inmate.
But he was also a German, and he was a-- he was a killer.
And of course, he called me.
He stopped me.
He was walking, and he stopped me.
And he raised his hand to hit me.
And he noticed my number, which we have in here.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And he looked at my number, and he says, you old-- called me bandit.
He says, you should know better than running now.
It's curfew.
I just told him the truth.
I said, I went to visit my brother.
And he lowered his hand, but he never hit me.
He says, go.
This time you can go.
And so they respected-- big deal-- they respected the guys that spend a year, a year and a half in the camps already.
I mean that, hey, if you could survive all this time and still be alive, there's something to it.
So he didn't hit me.
And they would hit you.
I mean, you could never forget this.
Or sometimes, even kill you.
So they respected the-- all the-- all the [NON-ENGLISH].
And suddenly before [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] time, they also had, as a matter of fact, in there-- the camps had women, but not for the Jewish inmates.
They had a special barrack where they had women for the men, but only for the other nationalities, Polish or German.
They were really mostly actually for the-- because there were quite a few German inmates also, prisoners.
So they would bring in and meet them there, the women for them, but not for the Jewish prisoners in there.
Just before the end, I-- I took sick.
And I was taken in to the hospital in Monowitz, in Buna.
There was a hospital.
The first time I was taken in was I lost feelings in my fingers.
They were just numb, completely numb, and wouldn't come back.
And of course, if they would know about it, they would-- I would be taken immediately out of the workforce and into the gas chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's someone who knew someone.
As I said, I've been in the camps for so long, that they took me in right after work.
Again, it's experimental.
But even though the doctors were also inmates in there, there were actual physicians that were inmates.
And I remember what they have done.
They have put on me a certain-- it's like a certain chain made out of metal or so.
And they had it connected.
It was-- it had electricity to it.
And then they would probe my veins in my-- in my back someplace, and my neck.
And every time they would hit a certain-- a certain part of it, you could see my fingers straighten out.
Like they would shoot electricity to it, and it would straighten out.
And after about three or four times, I was lucky.
This went away.
And of course, I was the happiest man on earth.
Then, just before the evacuation, it must have been January.
I had some tremendous, very large boils on my back.
And they had to be drained.
Of course, they had to be cut open.
They just-- they just wouldn't go away.
And as I said, I was a clean fellow.
I was-- didn't matter, winter, summer, I would run.
I would be the first one there.
Today, I mean, wintertime, I wouldn't dare go out.
And I would go out half naked.
I mean, I would run into the-- and this was quite a bit to run.
I'd say it must have been about 300 yards to run to-- to the bathhouse.
But I would do this.
Anyway, I was taken into the hospital.
And once you go to a hospital, of course, you lose your entire identity.
You're no more of this barrack.
So this barrack gets one piece of bread less, less soup, whatever it is.
It's now marked and whatever it is in there, I am now at-- at a hospital.
And while in the hospital, my brother comes to visit me one day.
I don't know how long I could have been there, maybe a day, two, three, four.
I-- I really can't remember time.
And he says, Ben, they are going to evacuate the camp.
The Russians are close by.
And they're evacuating the camp.
So anyway, he says, I'm going to bring you some clothes.
Because once you're in a hospital, you have no more clothes.
They're taken away from you and-- because you're in a hospital.
So he brought me some clothes.
And the following day, I just left.
Because I knew he said they are evacuating the camp today.
So I just left.
As it happened, which I found out after the liberation, that-- they didn't have time.
We-- we knew that what-- all the hospitals, they're going to kill everybody in the hospital.
They don't want no trace to be leftover what they were doing in hospitals with them.
So I left the hospital in the morning.
And of course, I had no bread.
I had no nothing.
And the camp was being evacuated.
Everybody got a portion of bread.
And I had no actual place where to go, because nobody had my piece of bread.
And I went to the-- to the block that I lived, that I was on before.
And in-- anyway, I got a big piece of bread.
Could have been a quarter of a pound or something, a thick slice of bread, that's all.
And we-- they evacuated that camp.
To the best of my recollection, Buna was liquidated on January-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] I believe it was January 18.
This is a date somehow which sticks in my mind.
Could have been maybe a day later or so, a day before.
I have no idea.
But they evacuated all the camps.
They evacuated Auschwitz.
And there were, as I said, satellite camps all the way around us.
There was [NON-ENGLISH].
There was [NON-ENGLISH].
There was-- I don't know how many other camps in there, but quite a few.
I mean, there were-- we were in a district where there were coal mines and salt mines.
And the prisoners were working in the salt mines and the coal mines.
So they evacuated all those camps.
And as a matter of fact, on the way [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as we were marching, and this I didn't know, of course, until years later when I came to the States.
But it's just a little interesting episode.
One of the guys who was with me in Buna, the SS men gave him his backpack to carry.
He didn't want to carry it.
Hey, somebody who can carry his backpack.
So he came to me.
And he says, hey, I got a backpack.
Let's move away from here and open it up.
And we took out the bread, and threw away the rest of the stuff.
And then this guy was looking for us.
Was looking for him, actually, not for me.
But he never found us.
He never found us, of course.
And we were marching by the thousands.
I mean, you couldn't look at every face in there.
And we arrived to a certain city.
That day, we marched.
And this was a death march.
I was at two death marches.
And whoever was left behind, of course, was shot.
In Poland, the winters, like any place, but especially in Poland, it was cold, bitter cold, I mean, that night.
So many people died that night.
We arrived to a certain station, to a certain city called Gleiwitz And this is where the railroad-- they had a-- a-- a large railroad station in there.
Even there, they had selections that day.
They took people to the right and to the left.
After we left, I'm quite sure that nobody would survive from-- from whoever they didn't want to send to Buchenwald.
Because what's the use in sending you someplace when you're half dead already?
So they just left these people.
They probably machine gunned them there.
And they gave us, I guess, a piece of bread, and they put us on the boxcars again.
And we were evacuated from there to Buchenwald.
Even that one night that we spent in that-- at that station in there, I witnessed these horrors.
I mean, these Germans, I presume you can't do it when you're sober.
So the-- a guy was going around with a hatchet, and just splitting the guys heads, just killing them right there.
He was drunk, and he was just-- And it was nighttime.
The following morning, we were on the train, again, in the boxcars leaving for Germany.
We didn't know where we were going.
As it happened, we went to Buchenwald.
We arrived in Buchenwald.
Again, this time, the boxcars were open.
And again, we're talking about January of 1945.
We arrived in Buchenwald.
Again, we were entered to a camp.
At that Buchenwald was strictly a repatriation camp.
It was a camp from which you were sent to other camps.
It was just a station to go camps.
We arrived in Buchenwald, and over there, we have gotten acquainted with an inmate-- I have to tell this-- arriving in Buchenwald with a friend from Belgium.
Who was a partisan and he was arrested as such.
Not as a Jew, but he was arrested as in the resistance.
Don't ask me why they didn't kill him right away, but they sent him to a concentration camp.
And he arrived to the camps probably in about 19-- either it was at the end-- in the beginning of '44, or sometime during 1944.
And we have kind of adopted him.
We already, as I've indicated before, I was singing in a choir.
I had an extra bowl of soup.
We had the bread from that civilian guy.
So I could-- we helped him.
He was my brother's age, and my brother was 11 years older than I am.
And kind of took a liking to him.
And I believed that this man, at one time, was telling us a story that he came to the United States.
But he was smuggled in on a boat.
And of course, he was repatriated, and he had to go back in that.
So we kind of took a liking to him, and he took a liking to us.
And we helped him out with a little extra soup, a little extra bread in there.
And he arrived in Buchenwald with us together.
When he arrived in there, Buchenwald had a tremendous underground movement in the camp.
The guys-- there were quite a few-- commanders from the underground, which were in Buchenwald.
As a matter of fact, I do have certain documents from these commanders who have given me witnesses, I guess.
When I finally left Belgium, I had to have some papers for the government to show them that I was on my best behavior in the camps.
I mean, if I could behave otherwise in there.
So anyway, when we came to the camps, they-- they locked him up immediately.
They had a roster.
They knew who was arriving in camps.
And they came to this man.
They came to this David.
His name was David Stern.
They said, David, everybody who has arrived within a week or so, they'll all be gone.
They're going to other camps.
But we can take you out.
We are organized in here.
And he told us this afterwards.
And we can keep you in here, no problem.
He says, I can't go without three other people.
That was me, my brother-- I, my brother, and there was another guy who happens to be now in Israel, who lives in Israel now.
He says, they have to go with me.
He says, David, you know the laws and the rules.
We can vouch for you.
And I know that you say you can vouch for them, but it doesn't work that way.
He says, we know you.
You know him From the camps, but you don't know him, really.
He says, they can't come in.
There's not even a question of them coming into the camps-- to the camp.
They're going to be sent away.
So he says, then I go with them.
I don't want to be separated from them.
They saved my life up to now.
He says, I'm going to stay with them.
They send us from there to a camp called Langenstein.
They called it [GERMAN], [GERMAN].
That was 10 kilometers away from a city by the name of Halberstadt This was pure hell.
Nobody ever came out of there.
They were just bringing in new prisoners.
Nobody-- they had no crematoriums in there, but they had a tremendous pit where-- where everybody was thrown in there.
And we were in that camp.
Almost no food at all.
What we were doing is working.
They called it [GERMAN], you know, it's [GERMAN], which means two mountains.
The German industry, towards the end of the war, was going underground.
And they were building factories underneath huge mountains or inside the mountains, so no bomb could penetrate in there.
And we were digging in the tunnels.
It took us maybe 10 guys to hold up a drill to drill.
There was no food given to us at all, just a little hot water or so.
And we were dying just like the flies.
And this particular man, David, he was already on his last round really.
He couldn't go to work anymore.
They wouldn't even waste a bullet to kill him.
Because, hey, bullets were hard to come by.
And they would just let him stay in the barrack and die.
They just wouldn't give him anything at all, no piece of bread, no food, nothing.
Well, he was in the barracks for about two days.
That third day, we went to work.
And they chased us out from the tunnel.
They said the Americans are nearby someplace.
The Russians-- the Americans-- no, it was the Americans, I believe.
So they evacuated this camp sometimes in the-- I would say it was April 9, April 10, April 8, of 1945.
So we weren't at that camp long.
First of all, nobody ever survived that camp long, because this was-- because this was the end.
As a matter of fact, most of the people which-- there was the uprising in Poland.
They were brought to that camp.
But of course, nobody survived that camp.
It was just an extermination camp, dying for work.
I mean, you just went, and you were pushing your cart with rocks.
And you just fell.
That was it.
That was the end of you.
You just run out of gas.
And that was it.
So they-- so, oh, and we told this David that they're evacuating the camp.
It's like giving him a new breath of life.
We-- he-- he just got up.
And he says, we might survive here.
They're evacuating the camp.
Anyway, that particular day, immediately, they just-- we went on a death march.
And most of the people, really, most of them, I would say died on that death march.
Because there was absolutely no food, no water, nothing given to us.
We were just marching deeper into Germany.
The-- the-- the joint forces were advancing, the American forces.
And we were just being driven in to Germany.
On the 14th of April, 1945, I could not-- I could function anymore.
It was being left on the side.
And if I am left, of course, I get the bullet.
And that's it.
So I said, I can't walk anymore.
I haven't got the strength.
At night, we would pick grass and eat grass, and put it inside our-- our jackets.
And-- and save it for the day to eat grass.
And that's all.
But there was absolutely no food given to us at all.
I remember stopping one-- one day to rest.
And for some reason, they picked a guy.
And I don't know.
Maybe he tried to steal something or whatever.
And they just-- it was a little river, I remember.
And they just, the German, just shot him point blank.
And I remember that most of the guys went-- they went into his pockets to see whether he's got anything to eat maybe, that guy.
So this was just the end.
So on April 14th, 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not being able to walk anymore.
And I remember we were wearing wooden shoes also.
And no food, no water, I said, this is the end.
I can't walk anymore.
So either I run, take my chances.
And during resting-- the rest, believe me, wasn't for us.
The rest was for the SS because they had to walk.
They had to guard us, supposedly, walking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, as we were getting ready to get up and walk away, a few of tank shells went through our-- our lines.
And a few people got killed, including one SS got killed in there.
So we knew the Americans are very close, not far away.
We started marching, and it was getting dark outside.
And we were getting towards the outskirts of a little town.
And I had to make up my right then and there.
Although my brother was the older one, and he always kept on saying-- he says, the Americans are very close.
They'll catch up to us.
Why run to them?
If you run, you get killed.
So I was younger.
I guess I was more daring.
Anyway, so I said, no, I can't walk anymore.
It's not the matter anymore whether I want to or not.
I gotta run.
So I was trying.
You know, it was getting dark.
We were getting-- leaving a town.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And we already passed the last road coming into town or a side road.
And I decided this is it.
It's getting dark.
I better start planning my run now.
And of course, that gentleman who happens to be now in Israel, he was always holding my hand.
He would never part with me.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS]
Joshua, Joshua Posniaka.
And I started plotting.
I said, we're going to run now.
And the SS, our guards, supposedly, well, they were supposed to be like every 50 feet or every 50 yards one.
But they always liked to talk to each other.
So two of them would come up together, you know, and talk.
So I figured it was about 100 yards now, or 100 meters, or whatever it is.
And then there could have been three guys just talking together.
And I figured, boy, I'll get away as far from them as I possibly can.
So their aim wouldn't be as good.
And then when it started just a little bit dark, I decided to run.
That Joshua was with me holding my hand.
My brother didn't run.
And I started calling him.
We called him Leon.
His name is [NON-ENGLISH].
But I called him Leon in the camps.
And I said, Leon, Leon.
But I called him [NON-ENGLISH], also [NON-ENGLISH].
And he finally decided at the last second.
He-- he-- I was already far away from them that he decided to run.
But as I was calling him, they-- they heard.
The Germans suddenly realized somebody is running away.
And they were shooting, but we were far away, far away already.
And they had to also make up their minds, the Germans.
Do they run after us, and leave thousands of people free to run?
And they figured, well, it's only three guys.
Let them go.
Well, we ran into the fields.
And of course, we were so exhausted.
I mean no food and anything else, and we noticed that everybody else was leaving, going away.
And we just fell asleep right in there.
And when we woke up in the morning, we were right outside a cemetery and a road.
I mean, we were probably lucky, because we were wearing our striped uniforms.
Everybody knew we were runaways from concentration camps, of the march.
But probably lucky nobody took this particular road, or that particular night in there, that road.
And the power of hunger was so great that you really didn't care whether you live.
But don't let me die on an empty stomach.
We were so hungry that if we can get some food-- so we decided to go into the village.
There was a village nearby.
Well, there's a cemetery.
There has to be a village someplace close by.
So we walked in.
There were three, four of us.
There was a kid from-- who's in Israel, Joshua, and there was David, David Stern.
And I remember we were approaching.
So before we went in, we just got into the yard.
Pigs were being-- pigs were eating.
And I remember my brother just ran to the pigs.
And whatever they had, he just hushed them away, grabbed their food, and he was eating it.
I went to the window, and here comes out that Nazi with his-- with his swastika band on him.
And he tells his wife in German.
We're telling him we are Czechoslovakian.
We don't understand German.
We don't speak German, just a few words.
And I hear him saying to his wife in German.
Give him some bread, but don't let him get away from here.
And if they want to go away, give them some more bread.
I'm going to look for the gendarmes.
When we heard this, you know, bread for the first time in six days, five days, bread, a piece of bread, whatever they gave us, and we would swallow it in seconds.
And-- and we knew that what-- what he told her.
We were ready to go away.
She says no, wait, wait, wait, that she would bring out more bread and more bread.
He came back.
In about five minutes, we must have each had I don't know how much bread.
But we had bread and water too.
And he says to her, everybody escaped.
There's no-- there's no authority in the city left anymore.
All the gendarmes are gone.
All the police, militia, is gone.
The Americans are three kilometers away.
And he mentioned the town, which I don't know what it was, the little city.
Of course, we heard this, so that Nazi German says still to us.
Look, he says, you're in no position to walk.
You can't walk, he says.
So we're going to do something for you.
This morning, he says, there was a international prisoners of war here, a whole group of them.
And they were walking in that direction.
I know that you can't walk, so we'll put you on a horse and cart, he says.
And one of us will drive you there.
Fine, we don't have to walk.
International prisoners, I figured that's a little bit better than inmates from concentration camps.
We immediately got a hold of some clothes.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] When they put us on this wagon, there were two other guys.
Probably the same as us, but nobody would talk.
And he had some clothes there.
These guys had some clothes.
We took the clothes from him.
We didn't even ask him, because we realized who he is.
And we changed right away.
And as our luck would be, we still caught up with a group of international prisoners.
There were British soldiers, French soldiers, Belgium soldiers.
There were some from the Slavic countries.
And there were few Russians also.
Don't ask me why Russians, but there were a few Russians in there too.
So they brought us to a point where the international prisoners of war couldn't walk anymore.
They were tired.
And they said, the British soldiers said, we can't walk anymore.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We are going to rest in here.
It was just on the outskirts of a town.
Yet everything around us was burning.
The planes, I'm quite sure they were Americans, kept lowering.
They were going lower, and lower, and lower.
And then the Germans came to the British people.
And they said, we cannot feed you here.
The best we can do is just bring you some water.
You see, they were treated a little bit better than-- they called us the political prisoners and the Auschwitz Buchenwald prisoners.
So they said fine, just give us water.
They already knew that the Americans are so close by because everything around us was burning.
And then the-- This is the end of tape number three.
